question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2025

Code: 65/2/3 (set-3)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  3. Please check that this question paper contains 38 questions.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This Question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Question paper is divided into FIVE Sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A - Question Number 1 to 18 are Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) and Question Number {tex}19 \& 20{/tex} are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B - Question Number 21 to 25 are Very Short Answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C - Question Number 26 to 31 are Short Answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D - Question Number 32 to 35 are Long Answer (LA) type questions, carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E - Question Number 36 to 38 are case study based questions, carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section - B, 3 questions in Section - C, 2 questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{D}{/tex} and {tex}{2}{/tex} questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{E}{/tex}.
  9. Use of calculator is not allowed.

SECTION - A

This section comprises of {tex}\mathbf{2 0}{/tex} multiple choice questions (MCQs) of {tex}\mathbf{1}{/tex} mark each.{tex}(20 \times 1=20){/tex}
1.

If {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{q}{/tex} are unit vectors, then which of the following values of {tex}\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q}{/tex} is not possible?

a. {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
d. {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
1
2.

Which of the following can be both a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix?

a. Unit Matrix
b. Diagonal Matrix
c. Null Matrix
d. Row Matrix
1
3.

If {tex}\int_{0}^{{a}} x {~d} x \leq \frac{{a}}{2}+6{/tex}, then

a. {tex}-4 \leq {a} \leq 3{/tex}
b. {tex}{a} \geq 4, {a} \leq-3{/tex}
c. {tex}-3 \leq {a} \leq 4{/tex}
d. {tex}-3 \leq {a} \leq 0{/tex}
1
4.

If {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} are square matrices of same order, then {tex}\left(A B^{T}-B A^{T}\right){/tex} is a

a. symmetric matrix
b. skew-symmetric matrix
c. null matrix
d. unit matrix
1
5.

The value of {tex}\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\cot ^{-1}(-\sqrt{3})\right){/tex} is

a. -1
b. {tex}\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
c. 0
d. 1
1
6.

If {tex}p{/tex} and {tex}q{/tex} are respectively the order and degree of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{d}}{{d} x}\left(\frac{{dy}}{{d} x}\right)^{3}=0{/tex}, then {tex}({p}-{q}){/tex} is

a. 0
b. 1
c. 2
d. 3
1
7.

The function {tex}{f}(x)=x^{2}-4 x+6{/tex} is increasing in the interval

a. {tex}(0,2){/tex}
b. {tex}(-\infty, 2]{/tex}
c. {tex}[1,2]{/tex}
d. {tex}[2, \infty){/tex}
1
8.

The line {tex}x=1+5 \mu, {y}=-5+\mu, {z}=-6-3 \mu{/tex} passes through which of the following point?

a. {tex}(1,-5,6){/tex}
b. {tex}(1,5,6){/tex}
c. {tex}(1,-5,-6){/tex}
d. {tex}(-1,-5,6){/tex}
1
9.

The area of the shaded region represented by the curves {tex}{y}=x^{2}, 0 \leq x \leq 2{/tex} and y-axis is given by

a. {tex}\int_{0}^{2} x^{2} {~d} x{/tex}
b. {tex}\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{y} d y{/tex}
c. {tex}\int_{0}^{4} x^{2} {~d} x{/tex}
d. {tex}\int_{0}^{4} \sqrt{y} d y{/tex}
1
10.

If E and F are two events such that {tex}{P}({E}) \gt 0{/tex} and {tex}{P}({F}) \neq 1{/tex}, then {tex}{P}(\overline{{E}} / \overline{{F}}){/tex} is

a. {tex}\frac{{P}(\overline{{E}})}{{P}(\overline{{F}})}{/tex}
b. {tex}1-{P}(\overline{{E}} / {F}){/tex}
c. {tex}1-{P}({E} / {F}){/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{1-P(E \cup F)}{P(\bar{F})}{/tex}
1
11.

For the following probability distribution:

x -4 -3 -2 -1 0
P(x) 0.1 0.2 0.3 0.2 0.2

E(X) is equal to:

a. –1.8
b. 0
c. –1
d. –2
1
12.

If projection of {tex}\vec{a}=\alpha \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} on {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} is 4 units, then {tex}\alpha{/tex} is

a. -13
b. -5
c. 13
d. 5
1
13.

The equation of a line parallel to the vector {tex}3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and passing through the point {tex}(4,-3,7){/tex} is:

a. {tex}x=4 {t}+3, {y}=-3 {t}+1, {z}=7 {t}+2{/tex}
b. {tex}x=3 {t}+4, {y}={t}+3, {z}=2 {t}+7{/tex}
c. {tex}x=3 {t}+4, {y}={t}-3, {z}=2 {t}+7{/tex}
d. {tex}x=3 {t}+4, {y}=-{t}+3, {z}=2 {t}+7{/tex}
1
14.

If a line makes angles of {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\theta{/tex} with the positive directions of {tex}x, y{/tex} and z -axis respectively, then {tex}\theta{/tex} is

a. {tex}\frac{-\pi}{3}{/tex} only
b. {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} only
c. {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
d. {tex}\pm \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
1
15.

A factory produces two products X and Y. The profit earned by selling X and Y is represented by the objective function {tex}{Z}=5 x+7 {y}{/tex}, where {tex}x{/tex} and y are the number of units of X and Y respectively sold. Which of the following statement is correct?

a. The objective function maximizes the difference of the profit earned from products X and Y.
b. The objective function measures the total production of products X and Y.
c. The objective function maximizes the combined profit earned from selling {tex}X{/tex} and {tex}Y{/tex}.
d. The objective function ensures the company produces more of product X than product Y.
1
16.

If A denotes the set of continuous functions and B denotes set of differentiable functions, then which of the following depicts the correct relation between set A and B?

a.
b.
c.
d.
1
17.

Four friends Abhay, Bina, Chhaya and Devesh were asked to simplify {tex}4 {AB}+3({AB}+{BA})-4 {BA}{/tex}, where A and B are both matrices of order {tex}2 \times 2{/tex}. It is known that {tex}A \neq B \neq I{/tex} and {tex}A^{-1} \neq B{/tex}.
Their answers are given as:
Abhay: 6 AB
Bina: {tex}7 {AB}-{BA}{/tex}
Chhaya: 8 AB
Devesh : {tex}7 {BA}-{AB}{/tex}
Who answered it correctly?

a. Abhay
b. Bina
c. Chhaya
d. Devesh
1
18.

If {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} are square matrices of order {tex}m{/tex} such that {tex}A^{2}-B^{2}=(A-B)(A+B){/tex}, then which of the following is always correct?

a. {tex}{A}={B}{/tex}
b. {tex}{AB}={BA}{/tex}
c. {tex}{A}=0{/tex} or {tex}{B}=0{/tex}
d. {tex}{A}={I}{/tex} or {tex}{B}={I}{/tex}
1

ASSERTION - REASON BASED QUESTIONS

Direction: Questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion and Reason based questions. Two statements are given, one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.
19.

Assertion (A): Every point of the feasible region of a Linear Programming Problem is an optimal solution.
Reason (R): The optimal solution for a Linear Programming Problem exists only at one or more corner point(s) of the feasible region.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
20.

Assertion (A): A {tex}=\operatorname{diag}\left[\begin{array}{lll}3 & 5 & 2\end{array}\right]{/tex} is a scalar matrix of order {tex}3 \times 3{/tex}.
Reason (R): If a diagonal matrix has all non-zero elements equal, it is known as a scalar matrix.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1

SECTION - B

This section comprises 5 Very Short Answer (VSA) type questions of 2 marks each.
21.

Find the values of 'a' for which {tex}{f}(x)=\sin x-{a} x+{b}{/tex} is increasing on R.

 2
22.

Find: {tex}\int 2 x^{3} {e}^{x^{2}} {~d} x{/tex}.

 2
23.(i)

If {tex}x = {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{x - y}}{{x\log x}}{/tex}

 2

OR
(ii)

If {tex}{f}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}2 x-3, & -3 \leq x \leq-2 \\ x+1, & -2 \lt x \leq 0\end{array}\right.{/tex}
Check the differentiability of {tex}{f}(x){/tex} at {tex}x=-2{/tex}.

 2
24.

If {tex}|\vec{a}|=2,|\vec{b}|=3{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=4{/tex}, then evaluate {tex}|\vec{a}+2 \vec{b}|{/tex}.

 2
25.(i)

A vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} makes equal angles with all the three axes. If the magnitude of the vector is {tex}5 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units, then find {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}.

 2

OR
(ii)

If {tex}\vec{\alpha}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\beta}{/tex} are position vectors of two points {tex}P{/tex} and {tex}Q{/tex} respectively, then find the position vector of a point R in QP produced such that {tex}{QR}=\frac{3}{2} {QP}{/tex}.

 2

SECTION - C

This section comprises of 6 Short Answer (SA) type questions of 3 marks each.{tex}(6 \times 3=18){/tex}
26.(i)

If {tex}{y}=\log \left(\sqrt{x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)^{2}{/tex}, then show that {tex}x(x+1)^{2} {y}_{2}+(x+1)^{2} {y}_{1}=2{/tex}.

 3

OR
(ii)

If {tex}x \sqrt{1+{y}}+{y} \sqrt{1+x}=0,-1 \lt x \lt 1, x \neq {y}{/tex}, then prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d} x}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^{2}}{/tex}.

 3
27.

Let R be a relation on set of real numbers {tex}\mathbb{R}{/tex} defined as {tex}\{(x, y): x-y+\sqrt{3}{/tex} is an irrational number, {tex}x, y \in \mathbb{R}{/tex}} Verify {tex}R{/tex} for reflexivity, symmetry and transitivity.

 3
28.

Solve the following linear programming problem graphically :
Minimise {tex}{Z}=2 x+{y}{/tex}
subject to the constraints:
{tex}3 x+y \geq 9{/tex}
{tex}x+y \geq 7{/tex}
{tex}x+2 y \geq 8{/tex}
{tex}x, y \geq 0{/tex}

 3
29.(i)

A die with number 1 to 6 is biased such that {tex}{P}(2)=\frac{3}{10}{/tex} and probability of other numbers is equal. Find the mean of the number of times number 2 appears on the dice, if the dice is thrown twice.

 3

OR
29.(ii)

Two dice are thrown. Defined are the following two events A and B: {tex}{A}=\{(x, {y}): x+{y}=9\}, {B}=\{(x, {y}): x \neq 3\}{/tex}, where {tex}(x, {y}){/tex} denote a point in the sample space.
Check if events A and B are independent or mutually exclusive.

 3
30.(i)

Solve the differential equation {tex}2({y}+3)-x {y} \frac{{dy}}{{d} x}=0{/tex}; given {tex}{y}(1)=-2{/tex}.

 3

OR
(ii)

Solve the following differential equation:
{tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=4 x^{2} .{/tex}

 3
31.

If {tex}\int_{a}^{b}{/tex} x3 dx = 0 and {tex}\int_{a}^{b}{/tex} x2 dx = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}, then find the values of a and b.

 3

SECTION - D

This section comprises 4 Long Answer (LA) type questions of 5 marks each.
32.(i)

Find the length shortest distance between the lines: {tex}\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-9}{-3}{/tex}  and {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+15}{-7}=\frac{z-9}{5}{/tex}

 5

OR
(ii)

Find the image {tex}{A}^{\prime}{/tex} of the point {tex}{A}(2,1,2){/tex} in the line {tex}l: \overrightarrow{{r}}=4 \hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex}. Also, find the equation of line joining {tex}{AA}^{\prime}{/tex}. Find the foot of perpendicular from point A on the line {tex}l{/tex}.

 5
33.

Find: {tex}\int(\sqrt{\tan x}+\sqrt{\cot x}) {d} x{/tex}.

 5
34.

Using integration, find the area of the region bounded by the line {tex}{y}=5 x+2{/tex}, the {tex}x{/tex}-axis and the ordinates {tex}x=-2{/tex} and {tex}x=2{/tex}.

 5
35.(i)

Given {tex}{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}-4 & 4 & 4 \\ -7 & 1 & 3 \\ 5 & -3 & -1\end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}{B}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & -2 \\ 2 & 1 & 3\end{array}\right]{/tex}, find AB . Hence, solve the system of linear equations:
{tex}x-y+z=4{/tex}
{tex}x-2 y-2 z=9{/tex}
{tex}2 x+y+3 z=1{/tex}

 5

OR
(ii)

If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&0 \\ { - 2}&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \\ 0&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then find the value of A–1.

Using A–1, solve the system of linear equations: 
x - 2y = 10,
2x - y - z = 8,
-2y +z = 7

 5

SECTION - E

This section comprises 3 case study based questions of 4 marks each
36.

A school is organizing a debate competition with participants as speakers {tex}{S}=\left\{{S}_{1}, {~S}_{2}, {~S}_{3}, {~S}_{4}\right\}{/tex} and these are judged by judges {tex}{J}=\left\{{J}_{1}, {~J}_{2}, {~J}_{3}\right\}{/tex}. Each speaker can be assigned one judge. Let {tex}R{/tex} be a relation from set {tex}S{/tex} to {tex}J{/tex} defined as {tex}{R}=\{(x, {y}){/tex}: speaker {tex}x{/tex} is judged by judge {tex}{y}, x \in {~S}, {y} \in J\}{/tex}.

Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. How many relations can be there from S to J? (1)
  2. A student identifies a function from S to J as {tex}{f}=\left\{\left({S}_{1}, {~J}_{1}\right),\left({S}_{2}, {~J}_{2}\right)\right.{/tex}, {tex}\left.\left({S}_{3}, {~J}_{2}\right),\left({S}_{4}, {~J}_{3}\right)\right\}{/tex} Check if it is bijective. (1)
    1. How many one-one functions can be there from set S to set J? (2)

      OR

    2. Another student considers a relation {tex}{R}_{1}=\left\{\left({S}_{1}, {~S}_{2}\right),\left\{{S}_{2}, {~S}_{4}\right)\right\}{/tex} in set S. Write minimum ordered pairs to be included in {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex} so that {tex}{R}_{1}{/tex} is reflexive but not symmetric. (2)
 4
37.

Three persons viz. Amber, Bonzi and Comet are manufacturing cars which run on petrol and on battery as well. Their production share in the market is {tex}60 \%, 30 \%{/tex} and {tex}10 \%{/tex} respectively. Of their respective production capacities, {tex}20 \%, 10 \%{/tex} and {tex}5 \%{/tex} cars respectively are electric (or battery operated). Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. (a) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is an electric car? (2)

OR

  1. (b) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is a petrol car? (2)
  2. A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Comet? (1)
  3. A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Amber or Bonzi? (1)
 4
38.


A small town is analyzing the pattern of a new street light installation. The lights are set up in such a way that the intensity of light at any point {tex}x{/tex} metres from the start of the street can be modelled by {tex}{f}(x)={e}^{x} \sin x{/tex}, where {tex}x{/tex} is in metres.
Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. Find the intervals on which the {tex}{f}(x){/tex} is increasing or decreasing, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex}. (2)
  2. Verify, whether each critical point when {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is a point of local maximum or local minimum or a point of inflexion. (2)
 4

{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-a{/tex}

i.e., {tex}\cos x \geq a{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow a \leq-1{/tex}

Find: {tex}\int 2 x^{3} {e}^{x^{2}} {~d} x{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}I=\int 2 x^{3} e^{x^{2}} d x=\int 2 x x^{2} e^{x^{2}} d x{/tex}
Put {tex}{x}^{2}={t} \Rightarrow 2 {xdx}={dt}{/tex}
{tex}I=\int t e^{t} d t{/tex}
{tex}=t e^{t}-\int e^{t} d t{/tex}
{tex}=t e^{t}-e^{t}+C{/tex}
{tex}={e}^{{x}^{2}}\left({x}^{2}-1\right)+{C}{/tex}

If {tex}x = {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{x - y}}{{x\log x}}{/tex}

Solution:

We have, {tex}x = {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{{dx}}x = \frac{d}{{dx}}{e^{x/y}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {e^{x/y}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}(\frac{x}{y}){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 1 = {e^{x/y}}.\left[ {\frac{{y.1 - x.dy/dx}}{{{y^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {y^2} = y.{e^{x/y}} - x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.{e^{x/y}}{/tex}

Let {tex}{x} \in \mathbb{R}{/tex}. Then we know that {tex}{x}-{x}+\sqrt{3}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}, which is an irrational number.

We have {tex}\sqrt{3}, 2 \in \mathbb{R}{/tex} such that {tex}\sqrt{3}-2+\sqrt{3}=2(\sqrt{3}-1){/tex}, which is an irrational number
{tex}\Rightarrow(\sqrt{3}, 2) \in {R}{/tex}.
But, {tex}2-\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{3}=2{/tex}, which is a rational number.
Hence, {tex}\Rightarrow(2, \sqrt{3}) \notin {R}{/tex}.
Therefore, R is not symmetric.
Let {tex}-\sqrt{3}, \sqrt{3}, 2 \in \mathbb{R}{/tex} such that {tex}(-\sqrt{3}, \sqrt{3}),(\sqrt{3}, 2) \in {R}{/tex}.
But, {tex}(-\sqrt{3}, 2) \notin {R}{/tex}
Therefore, R is not transitive.

t = -2 and s = 2
thus,

{tex}d=\sqrt{(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{16+16+16}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{48}{/tex}
{tex}=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

{tex}d=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units
Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is
{tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex}

  2. Since, {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{3}{/tex} have been assigned the same judge {tex}J_{2}{/tex}, the function is not one-one. Hence, it is not bijective.

Three persons viz. Amber, Bonzi and Comet are manufacturing cars which run on petrol and on battery as well. Their production share in the market is {tex}60 \%, 30 \%{/tex} and {tex}10 \%{/tex} respectively. Of their respective production capacities, {tex}20 \%, 10 \%{/tex} and {tex}5 \%{/tex} cars respectively are electric (or battery operated). Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. (a) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is an electric car? (2)

OR

  1. (b) What is the probability that a randomly selected car is a petrol car? (2)
  2. A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Comet? (1)
  3. A car is selected at random and is found to be electric. What is the probability that it was manufactured by Amber or Bonzi? (1)
Solution:
  1. (i) (a) Let {tex}{A}={Amber}{/tex} manufactures the car
    {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car
    {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car
    {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is electric
    {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{155}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{31}{200}{/tex}

OR

    {tex}{B}={/tex} Bonzi manufactures the car
    {tex}{C}={/tex} Comet manufactures the car
    {tex}{E}={/tex} The selected car is a petrol car
    {tex}P(A)=\frac{60}{100}, P(B)=\frac{30}{100}, P(C)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}P(E)=P(A) \times P\left(\frac{E}{A}\right)+P(B) \times P\left(\frac{E}{B}\right)+P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{90}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{95}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{845}{1000}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{169}{200}{/tex}
  2. {tex}P\left(\frac{C}{E}\right)=\frac{P(C) \times P\left(\frac{E}{C}\right)}{P(E)}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{20}{100}+\frac{30}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}+\frac{10}{100} \times \frac{5}{100}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{50}{10000}}{\frac{1550}{10000}}=\frac{1}{31}{/tex}

A small town is analyzing the pattern of a new street light installation. The lights are set up in such a way that the intensity of light at any point {tex}x{/tex} metres from the start of the street can be modelled by {tex}{f}(x)={e}^{x} \sin x{/tex}, where {tex}x{/tex} is in metres.
Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. Find the intervals on which the {tex}{f}(x){/tex} is increasing or decreasing, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex}. (2)
  2. Verify, whether each critical point when {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is a point of local maximum or local minimum or a point of inflexion. (2)
Solution:
  1. {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex}
    For critical points, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x+\sin x=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=-\sin x{/tex}
    For x to be a critical point {tex}x \in(0, \pi){/tex}, hence, {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
    For all {tex}x \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing in {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
    For all {tex}x \in\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right], f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex}
    Hence, f is decreasing in {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex}
  2. {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a critical point
    {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x)+e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex}
    {tex}=2 e^{x} \cos x{/tex}
    {tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=-v e{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a point of local maximum.
